{{Short description|Rate at which electrical energy is transferred by an electric circuit}}
{{Infobox physical quantity
| name = Electric power
| image = 
| caption = Electric power is the product of [[voltage]] and [[electric current|current]], and may be expressed in real, reactive, and apparent forms in AC circuits.
| symbols = <math>P</math> (real), <math>Q</math> (reactive), <math>S</math> (apparent)
| SI_unit = <math>P</math>: [[watt]] (W), <math>Q</math>: volt-ampere reactive (var), <math>S</math>: volt-ampere (VA)
| dimension = <math>ML^2T^{-3}</math>
}}

'''Electric power''' is the rate, per unit [[time]], at which [[electrical energy]] is transferred by an [[electrical circuit|electric circuit]]. In the simplest case of a [[direct current]] (DC) circuit, the power <math>P</math> delivered to a component is the product of the [[voltage]] <math>V</math> across it and the [[electric current|current]] <math>I</math> through it:

:<math>P = VI</math>

Using [[Ohm's law]], this can also be expressed as:

:<math>P = I^2R = \frac{V^2}{R}</math>

where <math>R</math> is the [[electrical resistance]] in [[ohm]]s. The SI unit of power is the [[watt]] (W), equivalent to one [[joule]] per [[second]].

== Power in AC circuits ==
In [[alternating current]] (AC) circuits, where voltage and current vary sinusoidally, the instantaneous power <math>p(t) = v(t)i(t)</math> fluctuates at twice the line frequency. For a sinusoidal voltage <math>v(t) = V_0\sin(\omega t)</math> and current <math>i(t) = I_0\sin(\omega t - \phi)</math>, the instantaneous power is:

:<math>p(t) = V_{\text{rms}}I_{\text{rms}}\cos\phi - V_{\text{rms}}I_{\text{rms}}\cos(2\omega t - \phi)</math>

where <math>V_{\text{rms}} = V_0/\sqrt{2}</math> and <math>I_{\text{rms}} = I_0/\sqrt{2}</math> are the [[root mean square]] values.

== Real, reactive, and apparent power ==

=== Real power (active power) ===
'''Real power''' <math>P</math> (unit: [[watt]], W) is the average power that performs useful work — it is the portion that is converted to heat, light, or mechanical motion:

:<math>P = V_{\text{rms}}I_{\text{rms}}\cos\phi</math>

where <math>\cos\phi</math> is the [[power factor]]. Real power is what electric utilities bill customers for.

=== Reactive power ===
'''Reactive power''' <math>Q</math> (unit: volt-ampere reactive, var) represents energy that oscillates between the source and the reactive components (inductors and capacitors) without being consumed:

:<math>Q = V_{\text{rms}}I_{\text{rms}}\sin\phi</math>

Reactive power is necessary for establishing [[magnetic field]]s in motors and transformers (inductive) and [[electric field]]s in capacitors.

=== Apparent power ===
'''Apparent power''' <math>S</math> (unit: volt-ampere, VA) is the product of RMS voltage and RMS current, ignoring the phase angle:

:<math>S = V_{\text{rms}}I_{\text{rms}}</math>

It is related to real and reactive power by:

:<math>S = \sqrt{P^2 + Q^2}</math>

== The power triangle ==
The relationship among <math>P</math>, <math>Q</math>, and <math>S</math> forms the '''power triangle''' — a right triangle with:

{| class="wikitable"
|-
! Quantity !! Symbol !! Unit !! Position in triangle
|-
| Real power || <math>P</math> || W || Adjacent side
|-
| Reactive power || <math>Q</math> || var || Opposite side
|-
| Apparent power || <math>S</math> || VA || Hypotenuse
|-
| [[Power factor]] || <math>\cos\phi</math> || — || <math>P/S</math>
|}

The angle <math>\phi</math> is the phase angle between voltage and current.

== Complex power ==
Using [[phasor]] notation, complex power <math>\mathbf{S}</math> is defined as:

:<math>\mathbf{S} = \mathbf{V}\mathbf{I}^* = P + jQ</math>

where <math>\mathbf{I}^*</math> is the complex conjugate of the current phasor. This formalism unifies the description of power in AC circuits and is central to [[power system]] analysis.

== Three-phase power ==
{{Main|Three-phase electric power}}
In [[three-phase electric power|three-phase systems]], the total real power delivered to a balanced load is:

:<math>P = \sqrt{3}\,V_L I_L \cos\phi</math>

where <math>V_L</math> is the line-to-line voltage and <math>I_L</math> is the line current.

== Power in DC circuits ==
For pure DC, voltage and current are constant, so <math>\phi = 0</math> and <math>\cos\phi = 1</math>. All power is real:

:<math>P = VI = I^2R</math>

The energy dissipated over time <math>t</math> is <math>E = Pt = VIt</math>, measured in [[joule]]s (or [[kilowatt-hour]] for utility billing: 1 kWh = 3.6 MJ).

== See also ==
{{Portal|Physics}}
* [[Electrical impedance]]
* [[Power factor]]
* [[Three-phase electric power]]
* [[Ohm's law]]
* [[Joule heating]]
* [[Electric current]]
* [[Voltage]]

== References ==
{{Reflist|30em}}

[[Category:Electric power| ]]
[[Category:Electrical engineering]]
[[Category:Physical quantities]]
